title: Roast Venison Haunch
subtitle: A rich, lean joint marinated overnight and roasted to a deep red-pink centre
excerpt: Venison haunch is the back leg, sold bone-in or boned and rolled. It is the leanest of all red meat roasting joints and dries quickly if overcooked. A wine and juniper marinade overnight adds moisture and flavour; roast to 58-60°C for red-pink.
sourceNotes: Jane Grigson, English Food (1974): venison haunch with red wine and juniper. Traditional British estate cookery. Hugh Fearnley-Whittingstall, The River Cottage Meat Book (2004): modern venison roasting guidance.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The joint should marinate for at least 12 hours, ideally 24. Remove from the marinade 1 hour before roasting to come to room temperature.

glossaryTerms.definition: A seasoned liquid, usually containing acid (wine, vinegar, citrus), oil, and aromatics, in which meat is soaked before cooking. For venison, a wine and juniper marinade adds both flavour and some extra moisture to compensate for the lack of fat marbling.

The berries of the juniper shrub, dried and used as a spice in northern European game cookery. Their resinous, piney flavour has an affinity with venison and other game, and is standard in both marinades and sauces for the meat.

content.text: Venison haunch roasts like topside beef: lean, needing a thermometer rather than time alone, and unforgiving above 65°C. The difference is in the flavour; venison has a depth and iron richness that benefits from a wine and juniper
overnight. The marinade also provides the base for the sauce.
Pull the joint at 58-60°C for a deeply red-pink interior. Venison served at medium-well is dry and disappointing.
are the defining spice here; lightly crush them in a pestle before adding to the marinade to release the volatile oils.

items.prepNote: haunch, boned and rolled, or bone-in
rashers, to bard if the joint is lean
redcurrant jelly or blackcurrant jelly, to sweeten the sauce

content.text: , thyme, and bay leaves in a large non-reactive dish. Add the venison, turn to coat, cover, and refrigerate for at least 12 hours, turning once.
Remove the joint from the marinade 1 hour before cooking; reserve the marinade. Pat the meat dry. Rub with

. Lay bacon rashers over the joint if using. Heat the oven to 220°C (200°C fan) and roast for 20 minutes to colour the surface. Reduce to 180°C (160°C fan) and continue until the centre reads 58-60°C for red-pink, or 62°C for medium-pink. Begin probing after 40 minutes.
Transfer to a warm board and rest for 20 minutes. In the roasting pan, pour in the strained marinade and
. Scrape the fond and reduce by half over high heat. Stir in
and season. Strain into a warm jug. Carve the joint in thin slices across the grain.

rows.symptom: Meat is dry and brown throughout
rows.cause: Overcooked past 70°C, or joint was too lean without barding
rows.fix: Probe from the start of checking and pull immediately at 60°C. Bard lean joints with bacon or lard the flesh with thin strips of bacon fat.

rows.cause: Marinade reduced too quickly and the alcohol became harsh
rows.fix: Strain the marinade before adding to the pan and simmer gently for 5 minutes before adding stock. Redcurrant jelly balances bitterness.

rows.cause: Wild venison with a strong gamy character, or improperly hung
rows.fix: Marinade overnight masks this to some degree. Farmed venison is consistently mild; source from a reputable game dealer or farm shop.

rows.cause: Boned and rolled joint was not tied evenly, creating thick and thin sections
rows.fix: Ask the butcher to tie the joint to an even cylinder. Alternatively use the probe to track the thickest section.

content.text: Venison has been a British estate table meat for centuries, reserved historically for the landowner's table. It is now widely available through farm shops, specialist butchers, and supermarkets. The flavour is denser and more mineral than beef, well-paired with the sharp sweetness of redcurrant jelly and the piney note of juniper. Serve with celeriac mash or roast root vegetables.
